file_name: Kibriya et al. - 2025 - Melanoma lesion localization using UNet and explainable AI.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework based on a modified UNet encoder-decoder architecture to automate the segmentation and localization of melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images. The study also employs Explainable AI (XAI) algorithms, specifically GRAD-CAM and Occlusion Sensitivity, to analyze the model's decisions. The model was trained and validated on the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2016-2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel deep learning framework for melanoma lesion localization. It details original experimental methods, including the use of a modified UNet architecture, hyperparameter optimization, and cross-database validation on ISIC datasets (2016-2020), resulting in new quantitative performance metrics (e.g., 95% accuracy, Jaccard Index 0.95) and XAI visualizations.

title: Melanoma lesion localization using UNet and explainable AI
brief_summary: This study presents a lightweight, automated deep learning framework for the segmentation and localization of melanoma lesions in dermoscopic images. The proposed method utilizes a modified encoder-decoder architecture inspired by the UNet model, optimized for computational efficiency and robustness against artifacts like hair and markers. The model was trained on the ISIC-2016 dataset and evaluated using cross-database validation on ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 datasets. Performance was assessed using metrics including accuracy, Jaccard Index, and Boundary F1 Score, with the model achieving 95% accuracy and a Jaccard Index of 0.95 on the primary dataset. Additionally, Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM and Occlusion Sensitivity, were employed to visualize and interpret the model's decision-making process. The results demonstrate the framework's efficacy and robustness in accurately segmenting lesions despite variations in intensity, size, and presence of clinical artifacts.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on the segmentation and localization of melanoma lesions. The ISIC-2016 dataset used for training contains only melanoma lesion images. The model distinguishes between melanoma pixels and background (healthy skin) pixels.
The segmentation task involves classifying pixels into two classes: melanoma and background/healthy skin. Table 2 reports performance metrics specifically for 'Background' and 'Melanoma' classes.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma lesion pixels and background (healthy skin) pixels in dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The paper presents a deep learning framework for the 'localization' and 'segmentation' of melanoma lesions. The model uses a modified UNet encoder-decoder architecture to produce segmentation masks that distinguish between melanoma pixels and background pixels. Performance is evaluated using metrics specific to segmentation tasks, including Jaccard Index (IoU), Boundary F1 Score, and class-wise accuracy for 'Melanoma' and 'Background' classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2016
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) database from the ISBI challenge of skin lesion analysis toward melanoma diagnosis 2016. Contains dermoscopic images with melanoma lesions, varying complexities (pixel intensity, texture, size), and artifacts (blood vessels, clinical swatches, markers, hair).

use.testing: Used for primary testing and evaluation (379 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, Jaccard Index, and Boundary F1 Score.

ISIC-2016.notes: The model was trained on 900 images and tested on 379 images. It achieved 95% accuracy on the test set. Class-wise performance is detailed in Table 2. The model successfully segmented lesions despite artifacts like hair, clinical swatches, and markers.

ISIC-2017.notes: Cross-database validation performed on 600 images. Results are summarized in Table 5. The text states 88.4% accuracy, while Table 5 lists Global Accuracy as 0.86 and Mean Accuracy as 0.88. The table values are used for specific metrics.

ISIC-2018.notes: Cross-database validation performed on 1512 images. The text states 91% overall accuracy. Table 5 lists Global Accuracy as 0.93 and Mean Accuracy as 0.91. The table values are used for specific metrics.

Proposed Modified UNet.Result: Successful segmentation of melanoma lesions on randomly selected images, demonstrating robustness against variations in size and intensity.

ISIC-2019.notes: Quantitative metrics were not reported for this dataset. Visual results are shown in Fig. 11.

Proposed Modified UNet.Result: Successful segmentation of melanoma lesions on randomly selected images, demonstrating robustness against variations in size and intensity.

ISIC-2020.notes: Quantitative metrics were not reported for this dataset. Visual results are shown in Fig. 12.

architecture_used.pretraining: Null (Not reported; weights initialized using 'He' weight initialization approach)

architecture_details.total_stages: 3 encoder stages, 1 bottleneck (bridge) stage, 3 decoder stages
architecture_details.weight_initialization: He initialization for convolutional layers' bias terms

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing melanoma lesion images and their masks to 64x64 pixels for computational efficiency
No removal of external artifacts (such as hair, clinical swatches, markers, or blood vessels) to ensure robustness in clinical settings
conclusions: The proposed modified UNet framework achieves high accuracy (95%) and robust segmentation performance (Jaccard Index 0.95, Boundary F1 Score 0.92) on the ISIC-2016 dataset.
The model demonstrates strong generalization capabilities through cross-database validation, achieving 88.4% accuracy on ISIC-2017 and 91% accuracy on ISIC-2018.

The framework effectively handles clinical artifacts such as hair, clinical swatches, markers, and variations in lesion intensity and size without requiring explicit artifact removal during preprocessing.
The proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art systems (including SegNet, FRCNN, BCF-CNN, FrCN, and UNet++) in terms of accuracy and Jaccard Index on the ISIC-2016 dataset.
Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Grad-CAM and Occlusion Sensitivity, confirm that the model focuses on relevant lesion areas, enhancing transparency and trustworthiness.
The study validates the efficacy of a lightweight encoder-decoder architecture with a depth of 3, optimized via hyperparameter tuning (Adam optimizer, 3e-4 learning rate, batch size 128, 50 epochs).
important_remarks: The model was trained exclusively on the ISIC-2016 dataset, which contains only melanoma lesion images, potentially limiting generalizability to other skin lesion types or benign conditions.
Cross-database validation was performed on ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018, but quantitative metrics were not reported for ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020, relying instead on qualitative visual results.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance evaluation relied solely on descriptive metrics.

The study did not remove clinical artifacts (hair, markers, swatches) during preprocessing to test robustness, which may reflect real-world conditions but limits isolation of segmentation accuracy from artifact interference.
Input images were resized to 64x64 pixels, which is a low resolution for dermoscopic images and may result in loss of fine-grained boundary details critical for precise lesion localization.
The dataset size is small (1,279 total images, 900 training), which may increase the risk of overfitting despite the use of dropout and hyperparameter optimization.

The paper does not report the number of trainable parameters or computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time), making it difficult to fully assess the 'lightweight' claim beyond epoch/batch size details.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; the paper states cross-database validation was used but does not detail how random splits were handled within the ISIC-2016 training/test split to ensure no patient overlap.
The model is binary (melanoma vs. background) and does not address multi-class segmentation or classification of melanoma subtypes, limiting its clinical utility for differential diagnosis.
